1: A theme: the joy of pilgrimage to God's house is fulfilled and surpassed in the approach to God through Christ. Hebrews 12:22 — "you have come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem." Ephesians 2:18 — "through him we both have access in one Spirit to the Father." The gladness of going to the LORD's house is now the gladness of those who come to God through Christ.
2: A theme: standing within Jerusalem's gates. John 10:9 — "I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved." The gates of the earthly Jerusalem give way to the gates of the heavenly city that are never shut (Revelation 21:25). The pilgrim who stood within Jerusalem's gates now approaches the new Jerusalem through Christ, the only gate.
3: A theme: the city "bound firmly together" — the unity of the gathered people. John 11:52 — Christ died "to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad." The firmly bound city is a picture of the church that Christ builds (Matthew 16:18) — a community held together not by geography but by union with him.
4: A theme: "to give thanks to the name of the LORD." The name before which all nations bow is the name of Jesus — Philippians 2:10-11 ("at the name of Jesus every knee should bow... and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord"). The universal thanksgiving the psalm envisions is the thanksgiving that flows from every nation through Christ.
5: A shadow: "Thrones for judgment were set, the thrones of the house of David." The Davidic judgment throne is fulfilled in Christ the king-judge. Acts 2:30 — God swore to set "one of his descendants on his throne"; Revelation 3:7 — Christ holds "the key of David." He is both the ruler on David's throne and the appointed judge of all (Acts 17:31).
6: A theme: "pray for the peace of Jerusalem." Christ wept over Jerusalem because it did not recognize "the things that make for peace" in its time of visitation (Luke 19:41-44). He is the answer to the prayer — Ephesians 2:14 "he himself is our peace," the one who reconciles both Jew and Gentile to God and to each other.
7: A theme: "peace be within your walls and security within your towers." The security that eludes earthly Jerusalem is the security of the heavenly city. Hebrews 11:10 — Abraham "was looking forward to the city that has foundations, whose designer and builder is God." The walls that never fall are the walls of the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:12-19).
8: A direct: "Peace be within you!" — Christ's first words to his gathered disciples after the resurrection. John 20:19,21,26 — "Peace be with you." The peace that the pilgrim prays for Jerusalem's sake, Christ pronounces over his gathered community. The resurrection greeting is the definitive answer to the pilgrimage prayer.
9: A type: "for the sake of the house of the LORD our God, I will seek your good." Zeal for God's house drove Christ to cleanse the temple — John 2:17 ("Zeal for your house will consume me" = Psalms 69:9). His zeal climaxed in the cross, where he gave himself as the final offering, making himself both the temple and the sacrifice (John 2:19-21).
